case_name: State of New Hampshire v. Matthew Brousseau
transcript_text:
This is case 2024, 0625, state of New Hampshire versus Matthew Brousseau.

Good morning, attorney family, and I understand you reserved two minutes for a rebound.

I have.

Please proceed.

Thank you.

Good morning.

My name is Pamela Nielsen.

I'm here on behalf of Mr. Brousseau.

I've reached three issues.

This morning I plan to focus and talk about three points with respect to the conference

issue, but of course I'll take questions on any of the other issues that the court may have.

Regarding the confrontation issue, I want to make three points.

I want to address first and start with the plain language of our confrontation range and our

constitution and the plain meaning of those words.

Second, I want to talk about the importance of those words and what it means to the

truth-seeking process of a trial as described in our constitution.

Attorney family, can we sort of cut to the chase here?

The process that the trial court used in this case is identical, seems to me, to the process

that was used in Massachusetts in the Bergstrom case.

Is that right?

A two-way electronic appearance by the alleged victims of complainants?

I'm only pausing because I've read so many cases with one-way and two-way.

I can't be entirely sure that Bergstrom was two-way.

But Bergstrom is one of the closest cases to the situation that we have here.

And at least with respect to the technology that was used in this case, it was two-way

as opposed to one-way in State v. Warren, which the court decided last year.

But here, the technology did no better in terms of meeting the requirements of our constitution.

The plain language of our constitution, our drafters did not write that accused persons

have the right to see and hear witnesses.

They wrote me, witnesses, face to face.

And this court went through extensively in State v. Warren

what those words meant at the time our constitution was drafted.

And I think that they mean the same things they mean today.

Something very different than just seeing and hearing a witness.

And I know the court in State v. Warren used the dictionary from 1773,

the Dictionary of English Language at the time.

And the court pulled out some definitions from that dictionary.

You better understand what meet face to face means.

There was another definition I found in there when I was looking at that reference last week.

And that is when you're looking at other definitions for face to face

or to face in that dictionary.

Another definition was nakedly without the interposition of other bodies.

In other words, literally in the presence of an accused.

And that's what this court held in State v. Warren and that's what it should have hold here.

Did you have a question? I'm sorry.

No, I was just going to note that in that decision this court did say

that an individual accused of crime has the right to be in the presence of

and face the witnesses testifying at trial.

Absolutely.

The presence means something more than just the two people seeing each other

and hearing each other.

And at the time our drafters drafted the Constitution,

they understood a matter of human nature that we still understand today,

which is that it is more difficult to tell a lie about a person

when you are in their physical presence looking at them face to face.

Now, as Justice Scalia and other judges that are,

that have authored some of the decisions I referred to in my brief have noted,

we can't force the witness to look at an accused person when they testify.

But the fact that they don't or other mannerisms about their demeanor

and the delivery of their testimony tell the fact finder

something about credibility of what is being said.

In fact, in our instructions to juries,

we tell them not just to listen to the words of a witness,

but to watch the witness.

Everything a trier of fact may detect about how the testimony is being delivered

is part of the truth inducing effect of physical presence

and is part of the truth detecting process that the trier of fact has to go through

by having everybody in the same room.

And it's worth noting that Judge McLeod,

it may be obvious if Judge McLeod didn't have the benefit of the Warren decision

when he made his decision in this case.

Correct.

And I do want to note, I think it was Judge McLeod who was the trial judge,

but I think Judge Warren Steen had issued the pretrial ruling allowing the process.

When Judge McLeod took over the case for the trial,

he chose not to revisit the decision.

But you are correct.

At the time the decision was made and at the time of Mr. Perceau's trial,

State New Warren had not been decided.

The only thing the trial court had at the time of Mr. Perceau's trial

was the trial court's decision in State New Warren,

which followed the Sixth Amendment interpretation of confrontation

and the exception under the Sixth Amendment stated in Maryland v. Craig.

Is there any accommodation that would be constitutionally sound,

in your opinion, that could be made for a child victim survivor

who is traumatized to the degree as allegedly was the case here?

Well, I have several answers to your questions.

First, I don't know whether at some point there may be technology

that would allow something other than the physical presence.

The process here was not a proper accommodation that satisfied the constitution.

Well, let's set aside a technological accommodation.

Is there something that could be done physically in the courtroom

that could make an accommodation yet pass constitutional muster from your perspective?

I think in Commonwealth v. Bergstrom

or one of the other SJC cases out of Massachusetts

talked in one of the decisions,

talked about some things that can be done to help any witness

deal with the effects of having to testify in person

and whether that's having an counselor available outside the courtroom,

setting the courtroom up in a less formal way.

Definitely not doing anything that's going to interfere

with a witness though having to see the defendant

and be in the presence of the defendant

and testify not only under the gaze of the defendant

but also the trigger of that.

And I would also point out a couple things about trauma.

It can be traumatic to talk about something that actually happened.

It could also be equally traumatic to have to come into a courtroom

and tell a story you might not be sure actually is true

or tell a story that someone has pressured you to say.

It can be traumatic to come into a courtroom

and tell a lie that you know it's a lie.

So just because someone has a traumatic reaction

to what they have to say or what they're saying

doesn't necessarily mean it's true.

That's part of the truth-seeking process of the trial.

And trauma isn't something new.

Trauma for people or difficulty people

have testified in the physical presence

of an accused in a criminal trial

is something that existed at the time

our drafters chose the language that they did

in our Constitution.

And they also were making important decisions

about the fundamental principles

they wanted to stand in our Constitution

when someone is facing a loss of liberty.

And they chose very high standards

not just that witnesses have to testify

in the physical presence of an accused

but that there's a presumption of innocence

and the highest burden of proof.

And what those reflect is a fundamental understanding

and intent of the drafters of our Constitution

to make it very difficult to convict someone

who may be innocent

even knowing that there may be at times

the cost of making it very difficult

to prove a guilty person is guilty.

That is a constitutional choice.

That's been recognized by the U.S. Supreme Court

in other contexts.

And by this court, in State B. Howard

which this court cited in State B. Warren

this court said the interest the state might have

in protecting a certain class of people

must fall when it is up against

the rights that we have given a defendant

to defend himself in the truth-seeking process of a trial.

Attorney Thielen, I want to get to the records issue.

I want to get to the records issue.

If this case is remanded for a new trial

shouldn't we just ask the parties to re-litigate

that issue before the trial court

given that we now have a different standard

that's set forth in Zarella that wasn't applicable

at the time this case was tried

and whether or not the defense can establish

an essential need for records that were obtained

from private entities?

I think... I'm sorry.

I'm pausing because I think the issue here

was the court had already done an in-camera review

and I think Zarella dealt with the first phase

of an in-camera review process

which is whether third parties

whether there's a different standard for third-grades

to have to produce records to the court.

In this case the court already went through that process

and so this court in Zarella did not address

to my understanding or change the standard

for what the court should produce

after doing an in-camera review.

So I don't think necessarily by if this case is remanded

for a new trial that the court should go through

should undo whatever it did.

It had those records in camera.

But to the extent that the defense believes

there are additional records that should have been disclosed

or should be disclosed

why not let the trial court make that decision?

Certainly if it's going back

if the parties want to re-litigate

or make additional arguments

I think that would be the prerogative of the court

to entertain those.

I'm not saying...

I don't have a reason to argue what would be for those.

It just seems to me that we need a standard

for which there would apply for an appellate court

to re-review records that haven't been disclosed.

It's sort of speculative just to say

that the trial court may have aired

and therefore we should undergo that review

on our own.

Unfortunately because the parties

aren't privy to those records

the court here is the only body or entity

who can take a look at what the trial court had

and applying the standard outlining gag me

and that line of cases as to what should be disclosed

I think that this court is in its best position

as the trial court

and this court is in the best position

to assess whether the trial court

should have disclosed or that standard

any additional records.

So that would apply basically in any case

in which records were disclosed?

Or not disclosed.

Right.

Yes.

I do want to just point out

on a confrontation issue

just a couple things about what this jury knew

because I think it's important.

The jury did not know

what the complainant in this case

was even looking at at the time that she testified

all that we know from the record

and the jury didn't know this

but she was staring at a laptop.

The jury didn't know what she was seeing on the laptop

except when she was asked

at the end of direct examination

if she could identify the defendant in the courtroom

but who she could see in the courtroom

the jury had no idea.

The jury could only see her face on the screen

it's not clear that she could even see the jury

or knew that they were in the courtroom

and

I'm sorry, this is my red light.

Thank you.

Okay, thank you very much.

You reserved two minutes.

Thank you all.

segments.text: This is case 2024, 0625, state of New Hampshire versus Matthew Brousseau.
Good morning, attorney family, and I understand you reserved two minutes for a rebound.
I have.
Please proceed.
Thank you.
Good morning.
My name is Pamela Nielsen.
I'm here on behalf of Mr. Brousseau.
I've reached three issues.
This morning I plan to focus and talk about three points with respect to the conference
issue, but of course I'll take questions on any of the other issues that the court may have.
Regarding the confrontation issue, I want to make three points.
I want to address first and start with the plain language of our confrontation range and our
constitution and the plain meaning of those words.
Second, I want to talk about the importance of those words and what it means to the
truth-seeking process of a trial as described in our constitution.
Attorney family, can we sort of cut to the chase here?
The process that the trial court used in this case is identical, seems to me, to the process
that was used in Massachusetts in the Bergstrom case.
Is that right?
A two-way electronic appearance by the alleged victims of complainants?
I'm only pausing because I've read so many cases with one-way and two-way.
I can't be entirely sure that Bergstrom was two-way.
But Bergstrom is one of the closest cases to the situation that we have here.
And at least with respect to the technology that was used in this case, it was two-way
as opposed to one-way in State v. Warren, which the court decided last year.
But here, the technology did no better in terms of meeting the requirements of our constitution.
The plain language of our constitution, our drafters did not write that accused persons
have the right to see and hear witnesses.
They wrote me, witnesses, face to face.
And this court went through extensively in State v. Warren
what those words meant at the time our constitution was drafted.
And I think that they mean the same things they mean today.
Something very different than just seeing and hearing a witness.
And I know the court in State v. Warren used the dictionary from 1773,
the Dictionary of English Language at the time.
And the court pulled out some definitions from that dictionary.
You better understand what meet face to face means.
There was another definition I found in there when I was looking at that reference last week.
And that is when you're looking at other definitions for face to face
or to face in that dictionary.
Another definition was nakedly without the interposition of other bodies.
In other words, literally in the presence of an accused.
And that's what this court held in State v. Warren and that's what it should have hold here.
Did you have a question? I'm sorry.
No, I was just going to note that in that decision this court did say
that an individual accused of crime has the right to be in the presence of
and face the witnesses testifying at trial.
Absolutely.
The presence means something more than just the two people seeing each other
and hearing each other.
And at the time our drafters drafted the Constitution,
they understood a matter of human nature that we still understand today,
which is that it is more difficult to tell a lie about a person
when you are in their physical presence looking at them face to face.
Now, as Justice Scalia and other judges that are,
that have authored some of the decisions I referred to in my brief have noted,
we can't force the witness to look at an accused person when they testify.
But the fact that they don't or other mannerisms about their demeanor
and the delivery of their testimony tell the fact finder
something about credibility of what is being said.
In fact, in our instructions to juries,
we tell them not just to listen to the words of a witness,
but to watch the witness.
Everything a trier of fact may detect about how the testimony is being delivered
is part of the truth inducing effect of physical presence
and is part of the truth detecting process that the trier of fact has to go through
by having everybody in the same room.
And it's worth noting that Judge McLeod,
it may be obvious if Judge McLeod didn't have the benefit of the Warren decision
